title: Classic Spaghetti Carbonara
intro: Silky ribbons of spaghetti coated in a glossy egg and cheese sauce studded with crisp guanciale. This Roman classic relies on heat and timing rather than cream for its luxurious finish.
ingredients: 150 g guanciale, cut into small strips
Fine salt for the pasta water
steps: Bring a large pot of well-salted water to a rolling boil and cook the spaghetti until just al dente.
While the pasta cooks, fry the guanciale in a cold dry pan over medium heat until the fat renders and the strips turn golden and crisp.
Whisk the egg yolks, whole egg, both cheeses and the black pepper together in a bowl until smooth.
Reserve a mugful of starchy pasta water, then drain the spaghetti and add it to the pan with the guanciale, off the heat.
Pour in the egg mixture and toss vigorously, adding splashes of the reserved water until a creamy sauce forms.
Serve immediately in warm bowls with an extra dusting of Pecorino and more cracked pepper.
tip: Always add the eggs off the heat so they thicken into a sauce instead of scrambling.

title: Tagliatelle al Ragu Bolognese
intro: A deeply savoury meat sauce simmered slowly with milk and wine until rich and velvety. Traditionally cloaked over fresh tagliatelle rather than spaghetti for the perfect cling.
ingredients: Salt, pepper and grated Parmesan to serve
steps: Warm the olive oil in a heavy pot and gently soften the onion, carrot, celery and pancetta until fragrant.
Raise the heat, add the beef and pork, and brown thoroughly while breaking up any clumps.
Pour in the milk and simmer until it is almost fully absorbed, then repeat with the white wine.
Stir in the chopped tomatoes and tomato paste, season, and bring to a gentle bubble.
Lower the heat and simmer uncovered for at least two and a half hours, stirring occasionally until glossy and thick.
Cook the tagliatelle in salted boiling water until al dente, then toss with a few ladles of the ragu.
Plate up and finish each serving with plenty of grated Parmesan.
tip: The milk added early tenderises the meat and rounds out the sauce, so do not skip it.

title: Spaghetti Aglio e Olio
intro: A humble Neapolitan midnight favourite built from little more than garlic, good olive oil and chilli. Simple, fast and endlessly satisfying when you crave big flavour with few ingredients.
ingredients: Small handful of flat-leaf parsley, chopped
steps: Cook the spaghetti in a large pot of salted boiling water until al dente.
Meanwhile, warm the olive oil in a wide pan and add the sliced garlic over gentle heat.
Cook the garlic slowly until pale gold and fragrant, then stir in the chilli flakes.
Reserve a cup of pasta water, drain the spaghetti, and add it to the garlicky oil.
Toss well with a splash of the reserved water until the pasta glistens and emulsifies.
Fold through the parsley, adjust the salt, and serve at once with Parmesan if you like.
tip: Keep the heat low while cooking the garlic, as burnt garlic turns the whole dish bitter.

title: Penne alla Vodka
intro: A blushing tomato and cream sauce brightened by a splash of vodka that coaxes out deep, rounded flavour. Comforting yet a little elegant, it clings beautifully to ridged penne.
ingredients: Salt, pepper and fresh basil to serve
steps: Heat the olive oil in a large pan and soften the onion and garlic with the chilli flakes.
Pour in the vodka and let it bubble for a minute or two to cook off the raw edge.
Add the crushed tomatoes, season, and simmer gently for around fifteen minutes until thickened.
Stir in the cream and warm through until the sauce turns a rich blush colour.
Cook the penne in salted boiling water until al dente, reserving a little pasta water.
Toss the drained pasta into the sauce with the Parmesan, loosening with pasta water as needed.
Finish with torn basil and an extra grating of cheese.
tip: Let the vodka simmer long enough to mellow, or the sauce can taste sharp.

title: Linguine alle Vongole
intro: Fresh clams steamed open in white wine and garlic, their briny liquor becoming the sauce itself. A coastal Italian classic that tastes of the sea with every forkful.
ingredients: 1 kg fresh clams, cleaned and purged
steps: Begin cooking the linguine in salted boiling water until just short of al dente.
Warm the olive oil in a large deep pan and gently cook the garlic and chilli flakes until fragrant.
Add the clams and the white wine, then cover and steam until the shells open, discarding any that stay shut.
Reserve a little pasta water, then lift the drained linguine straight into the pan with the clams.
Toss over the heat for a minute so the pasta drinks up the briny sauce, loosening with pasta water if needed.
Scatter over the parsley and serve immediately in warm bowls.
tip: Soak the clams in salted water beforehand so they release any grit before cooking.

title: Cacio e Pepe
intro: Just cheese, pepper and pasta transformed into a creamy Roman masterpiece through technique alone. The starchy water and Pecorino emulsify into a clinging, peppery sauce.
ingredients: 2 tsp whole black peppercorns, freshly ground
steps: Cook the pasta in a modest amount of well-salted boiling water so the starch stays concentrated.
Toast the freshly ground black pepper in a dry pan for a moment to release its aroma.
Ladle a little hot pasta water into the pan with the pepper and let it bubble gently.
In a bowl, mix the grated Pecorino with a splash of cooler pasta water to form a smooth paste.
Drain the pasta just before al dente and toss it in the peppery pan with more pasta water.
Remove from the heat, stir in the cheese paste, and toss until a glossy sauce coats every strand.
tip: Let the pasta water cool slightly before mixing with the cheese to avoid clumping.

title: Pappardelle with Slow-Braised Beef Ragu
intro: Tender beef shin braised in red wine until it falls into rich, silky shreds. Draped over broad pappardelle, it is the ultimate cold-weather comfort dish.
ingredients: 1 kg beef shin, cut into chunks
Salt, pepper and Parmesan to serve
steps: Season the beef generously and brown it in batches in a hot casserole with olive oil, then set aside.
Lower the heat and soften the onions, carrots, celery and garlic in the same pot.
Pour in the red wine and scrape up the browned bits, letting it reduce by half.
Return the beef with the tomatoes, stock and rosemary, then bring to a gentle simmer.
Cover and braise in a low oven for around three hours until the meat shreds easily.
Shred the beef into the sauce and adjust the seasoning until deeply savoury.
Cook the pappardelle until al dente, toss with the ragu, and finish with grated Parmesan.
tip: Brown the beef properly in batches, as crowding the pan steams the meat and dulls the flavour.

title: Pasta alla Norma
intro: A Sicilian celebration of golden fried aubergine, sweet tomato sauce and salty ricotta salata. Rustic, vibrant and named in honour of a beloved opera.
ingredients: 5 tbsp olive oil, plus extra for frying
steps: Salt the cubed aubergine and let it sit for fifteen minutes, then pat dry to draw out moisture.
Fry the aubergine in hot olive oil until golden on all sides, then drain on paper towels.
In a clean pan, gently cook the garlic in olive oil, then add the tomatoes and simmer until thickened.
Season the sauce, tear in most of the basil, and stir the fried aubergine back through.
Cook the rigatoni in salted boiling water until al dente and reserve a little pasta water.
Toss the pasta with the sauce, loosening with pasta water so it coats every piece.
Serve topped with grated ricotta salata and the remaining basil.
tip: Salting the aubergine first keeps it from soaking up too much oil while frying.

title: Fettuccine Alfredo
intro: Ribbons of fettuccine bathed in a glossy emulsion of butter and Parmesan. The original Roman version uses no cream, relying on cheese and starchy water for its richness.
steps: Cook the fettuccine in salted boiling water until just al dente, reserving plenty of pasta water.
Melt half the butter with a ladle of hot pasta water in a wide pan to form a milky base.
Add the drained pasta and the remaining butter, tossing until the butter melts and coats the strands.
Remove the pan from the heat and gradually shower in the grated Parmesan while tossing constantly.
Loosen with more pasta water as needed until a smooth, glossy sauce forms.
Season with black pepper and serve immediately while hot and creamy.
tip: Work off the heat when adding the cheese so it melts smoothly instead of turning stringy.

title: Orecchiette with Broccoli Rabe and Sausage
intro: Ear-shaped pasta from Puglia tossed with bitter greens, garlic and crumbled sausage. A rustic southern dish balancing savoury, spicy and pleasantly bitter notes.
steps: Blanch the broccoli rabe in salted boiling water for two minutes, then lift it out and roughly chop.
Cook the orecchiette in the same pot of boiling water until al dente.
Meanwhile, brown the crumbled sausage in olive oil until golden, breaking it into small pieces.
Add the garlic and chilli flakes, cooking until fragrant, then stir in the chopped broccoli rabe.
Reserve some pasta water, drain the orecchiette, and add it to the pan with the sausage and greens.
Toss everything together with a splash of pasta water, then finish with grated Pecorino.
tip: Blanching the broccoli rabe first tames its bitterness while keeping its bright colour.

title: Spaghetti alla Puttanesca
intro: A boldly flavoured Neapolitan sauce of tomatoes, olives, capers and anchovies. Punchy, salty and aromatic, it comes together quickly from pantry staples.
ingredients: 100 g black olives, pitted and halved
steps: Warm the olive oil in a pan and melt the anchovies with the garlic and chilli flakes until dissolved.
Stir in the olives and capers, letting them sizzle briefly to release their aroma.
Add the chopped tomatoes and simmer gently for around fifteen minutes until the sauce thickens.
Meanwhile, cook the spaghetti in salted boiling water until al dente.
Reserve a little pasta water, drain the spaghetti, and toss it through the sauce.
Loosen with pasta water if needed, scatter over the parsley, and serve at once.
tip: The anchovies, olives and capers are salty, so taste before adding any extra salt.

title: Trofie al Pesto Genovese
intro: Hand-rolled Ligurian trofie tossed with a fragrant basil pesto, potatoes and green beans. The vibrant sauce is pounded fresh for the truest, brightest flavour.
steps: Pound the basil, pine nuts, garlic and a pinch of salt into a paste with a mortar and pestle.
Work in both cheeses, then slowly stream in the olive oil until a loose green sauce forms.
Cook the diced potato in salted boiling water for a few minutes, then add the trofie and green beans.
Cook everything together until the pasta is al dente and the vegetables are tender.
Reserve a little pasta water, then drain the pasta, potato and beans together.
Toss with the pesto off the heat, loosening with pasta water until silky, and serve right away.
tip: Never heat the pesto directly, as high heat dulls its colour and fresh basil aroma.

title: Baked Ziti with Mozzarella
intro: Tubes of ziti folded into a hearty tomato sauce, layered with cheese and baked until bubbling. A crowd-pleasing bake with a golden, melty top and a soft, saucy centre.
steps: Soften the onion and garlic in olive oil, then add the passata and simmer until thickened and seasoned.
Cook the ziti in salted boiling water until just shy of al dente, then drain.
Toss the pasta with most of the tomato sauce, the ricotta and the torn basil until well coated.
Spoon half the mixture into a baking dish and scatter over half the mozzarella.
Layer in the remaining pasta, then top with the rest of the sauce, mozzarella and Parmesan.
Bake in a hot oven for around twenty-five minutes until golden and bubbling.
Let it rest for a few minutes before serving so the layers hold together.
tip: Undercook the pasta slightly since it continues softening in the oven as it bakes.
